title: Low-Observable Deployable Modular Surface Platform (LODMSP)
subtitle: From Fixed Decks to Deployable Mission Interfaces in Autonomous Maritime Systems
title_normalized: low observable deployable modular surface platform lodmsp

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher_unit: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center

alternate_identifiers.description: Working paper number printed in the PDF

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Low-Observable Deployable Modular Surface Platform (LODMSP) From Fixed Decks to Deployable Mission Interfaces in Autonomous Maritime Systems.pdf

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the Low-Observable Deployable Modular Surface Platform concept

description: This working paper introduces the Low-Observable Deployable Modular Surface Platform (LODMSP) as a conceptual maritime morphology for autonomous surface systems. It argues that fixed-deck modularity may be a transitional stage and that future platforms may increasingly be evaluated by their ability to compress into low-profile transit configurations and expand into deployable mission interfaces for sensing, communication, unmanned-system handling, payload separation, logistics support, and distributed maritime coordination.
abstract: This working paper introduces the Low-Observable Deployable Modular Surface Platform (LODMSP) as a conceptual maritime morphology for the next phase of autonomous surface systems. It argues that fixed-deck modularity may be a transitional stage as modular payloads, containerized capability, distributed maritime operations, and autonomous mission systems increasingly separate payloads from platforms. LODMSP is not presented as a specific ship design, weapons architecture, or acquisition recommendation. Rather, it is an analytical model for asking how platform architecture may evolve when low-observable transit, modular payload reconfiguration, expanded mission surfaces, and autonomous orchestration become simultaneous design priorities. In transit mode, the platform compresses into a compact, low-profile form optimized for movement, exposure reduction, and module protection. In mission mode, it expands radially, laterally, or segmentally to increase functional surface area, separate mission modules, enlarge sensor baselines, support unmanned-system handling, and distribute communication or logistics functions. The paper links this concept to public trends including DARPA NOMARS, USX-1 Defiant, the U.S. Navy’s containerized capability direction, MASC, Mk 70, and lessons from Littoral Combat Ship modularity. It concludes that LODMSP is best understood as a way of thinking about the next phase of autonomous maritime power: from fixed decks to deployable mission interfaces.

series_information.series_description: WP-A develops foundational concepts and diagnostic frameworks for AI governance, autonomous systems, and emerging-technology strategy, examining how power, risk, control, and institutional capacity are reorganized as AI systems become embedded in security, economic, and governance infrastructures.

methodology.framework_name: LODMSP Maritime Morphology and Deployable Mission Interface Assessment Framework
methodology.source_basis: Conceptual working paper based on public sources on DARPA NOMARS and USX-1 Defiant, Lockheed Martin Mk 70, NAVSEA Modular Attack Surface Craft, CRS reporting on Navy large USVs, GAO findings on Littoral Combat Ship modularity, U.S. Navy materials on MUSV and containerized capability, the author’s prior EPINOVA framework on morphological convergence and infrastructure power in autonomous systems, and public research on transformable USVs.
methodology.case_context: Autonomous surface systems, containerized naval capability, modular payload architectures, and distributed maritime operations.
methodology.core_problem: Whether future autonomous surface platforms should remain fixed decks carrying modular payloads or evolve into deployable mission interfaces that change physical form between transit and operational states.
central_claims: Fixed-deck modularity is likely a transitional stage in autonomous maritime systems.
Deployable geometry may become a maritime design frontier as platforms are evaluated by the ratio between compact transit form and expanded mission form.

LODMSP reframes the vessel from a carrier of payloads into a reconfigurable physical interface for sensing, communication, unmanned-system operations, payload spacing, logistics, maintenance access, and distributed coordination.
The concept aligns with public trends in NOMARS, MASC, containerized capability, and modular payloads while remaining distinct from known public designs.
The strategic value of LODMSP depends less on single-platform lethality than on orchestration, interfaces, sustainment, and distributed maritime ecosystem integration.

analytical_lenses.name: Fixed-Deck Modularity Assessment
analytical_lenses.description: Evaluates the limits of fixed decks, fixed mission bays, finite surface area, module proximity, and platform-centric thinking.

analytical_lenses.name: Deployable Mission Interface Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Assesses how compact transit morphology can transition into expanded mission surfaces for distributed sensing, communications, logistics, and unmanned-system operations.

analytical_lenses.name: Platform-Payload Decoupling Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Examines how containerized capability and modular payload trends separate mission systems from hull-specific architectures.

analytical_lenses.name: Infrastructure-Centered Autonomous Power
analytical_lenses.description: Links LODMSP to the author’s prior framework that long-term advantage shifts from body-level optimization to orchestration, modularity, infrastructure control, and system-level scalability.

analytical_lenses.name: Modularity Risk and LCS Cautionary Lens
analytical_lenses.description: Uses the LCS experience as a warning that modularity requires mature interfaces, sustainment planning, testing, training, and doctrine.

dimensions.name: Folded / Transit Configuration
dimensions.observed_pattern: Compact, continuous, low-profile transit morphology minimizes protrusions, exposed mission equipment, windage, and module exposure.
dimensions.interpretation: Optimized for movement, exposure reduction, and module protection rather than mission-surface availability.

dimensions.name: Deployed / Mission Configuration
dimensions.observed_pattern: Radial, lateral, or segmented mission surfaces expand to support sensing, communication, unmanned-system operations, logistics, and module separation.
dimensions.interpretation: The vessel becomes a deployable mission interface rather than a fixed carrier of payloads.

dimensions.name: Deployment Ratio
dimensions.observed_pattern: Mission-state functional area relative to transit-state footprint.
dimensions.interpretation: Tests whether compact transit form can generate meaningful operational surface after deployment.

dimensions.name: Functional Interface Ratio
dimensions.observed_pattern: Usable sensing, communication, handling, maintenance, or module interface relative to platform volume or displacement.
dimensions.interpretation: Shifts evaluation from vessel size to usable external interaction per unit of hull.

dimensions.name: Modular Separation Index
dimensions.observed_pattern: Ability to separate modules that interfere electronically, physically, thermally, or operationally.
dimensions.interpretation: Clarifies whether modularity reduces or simply relocates integration risk.

dimensions.name: Distributed Expansion Coefficient
dimensions.observed_pattern: Degree to which the platform can extend its operational footprint through UAVs, USVs, UUVs, floating sensors, relays, or logistics nodes.
dimensions.interpretation: Links platform design to distributed maritime operations and autonomous force orchestration.

dimensions.name: Interface Maturity Level
dimensions.observed_pattern: Reliability and standardization of power, data, cooling, mechanical locking, autonomy, and software-update pathways.
dimensions.interpretation: Prevents modularity from becoming a promise unsupported by integration and sustainment architecture.

key_findings: Fixed-deck modularity is likely transitional as modular payloads and mission modules become central but remain constrained by fixed decks and conventional seaframe assumptions.
Deployable geometry may become a maritime design frontier, especially the ratio between compact transit form and expanded mission form.
LODMSP reframes the vessel from carrier to interface, turning the platform itself into a reconfigurable physical surface for mission functions.
The concept aligns with public trends but remains distinct from known public designs such as NOMARS, USX-1 Defiant, MASC, and containerized naval payloads.
LODMSP’s strategic value would depend less on single-platform lethality than on orchestration, distributed maritime ecosystem integration, sustainment, and infrastructure support.
The LCS experience shows that modularity must be treated as system architecture rather than a procurement slogan.
policy_recommendations: Evaluate deployable mission surface as a design metric alongside traditional metrics such as range, payload, speed, endurance, and cost.

Separate concept exploration from weapons integration, studying LODMSP first as an architecture for sensing, communication, unmanned-system handling, logistics, maintenance, and distributed coordination.
Prioritize interface standards for data, power, cooling, mechanical locking, autonomy integration, software updates, module certification, and sustainment documentation.
Learn from LCS without abandoning modularity by ensuring mature mission packages, sustainment planning, training, and operational testing.
Analyze LODMSP as a node in distributed maritime operations rather than as an independent ship replacement.
Include governance, identification, command accountability, cyber resilience, signaling, classification, and lawful-use considerations early in design.

watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tests whether deployable geometry creates functional operational value without unacceptable mechanical complexity.

watchpoints.indicator: Interface maturity for power, data, cooling, mechanical locking, autonomy, and software-update pathways
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Determines whether modularity is supported by integration and sustainment architecture.

watchpoints.indicator: Maintenance burden, corrosion, fatigue, sealing, hinge reliability, and sea-state performance
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether moving maritime structures remain viable under operational conditions.

watchpoints.indicator: Rules for tasking, deploying, protecting, recovering, maintaining, and signaling variable-geometry autonomous platforms
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Identifies doctrine gaps before operational deployment.

watchpoints.indicator: Classification, command responsibility, cyber resilience, auditability, and lawful-use controls
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Reduces escalation and accountability risks associated with variable-configuration systems.

related_works.identifier: Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency. (n.d.). NOMARS: No manning required ship. https://www.darpa.mil/research/programs/no-manning-required-ship
related_works.description: Supports the discussion of unmanned-vessel design freedom created by removing onboard human accommodation requirements.

related_works.identifier: Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency. (2025, August 11). NOMARS at-sea demo. https://www.darpa.mil/news/features/nomars
related_works.description: Supports the discussion of USX-1 Defiant and long-duration autonomous at-sea demonstration.

related_works.identifier: Lockheed Martin. (2023). Mk 70 Mod 1 Payload Delivery System. https://www.lockheedmartin.com/content/dam/lockheed-martin/rms/documents/naval-launchers-and-munitions/Mk70_Product_Card.pdf
related_works.description: Supports the discussion of containerized high-capability naval payloads and the fixed-deck modularity baseline.

related_works.identifier: Naval Sea Systems Command. (2025, July 31). U.S. Navy seeks industry feedback for Modular Attack Surface Craft program. https://www.navsea.navy.mil/Media/News/Article-View/Article/4261138/us-navy-seeks-industry-feedback-for-modular-attack-surface-craft-program/
related_works.description: Supports the paper’s discussion of MASC, modular design philosophy, commercial off-the-shelf technology, and rapid fielding requirements.

related_works.identifier: O'Rourke, R. (2026, January 16). Navy large unmanned surface vessels (USVs): Background and issues for Congress (CRS Report No. R45757). Congressional Research Service. https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R45757
related_works.description: Supports the background discussion of Navy unmanned surface vessels, large USVs, and congressional policy issues.

related_works.identifier: U.S. Government Accountability Office. (2022). Littoral Combat Ship: Actions needed to address significant operational challenges and implement planned sustainment approach (GAO-22-105387). https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-22-105387
related_works.description: Provides the cautionary precedent for modularity, mission-module integration, sustainment, testing, and operational capability challenges.

related_works.identifier: U.S. Navy. (2025, August 27). Medium unmanned surface vessel (MUSV). https://www.navy.mil/Resources/Fact-Files/Display-FactFiles/Article/4288073/medium-unmanned-surface-vessel-musv/
related_works.description: Supports the discussion of Navy unmanned-vessel experimentation and integration into fleet operations.

related_works.identifier: U.S. Navy. (2026, April 20). CNO remarks at Sea-Air-Space. https://www.navy.mil/Press-Office/Speeches/display-speech/Article/4464830/cno-remarks-at-sea-air-space/
related_works.description: Supports the discussion of containerized capability, payload-platform decoupling, open architecture, digital twins, and modular effects across the fleet.

related_works.identifier: Wu, S. (2026). From control substitution to structural dominance: Morphological convergence and infrastructure power in autonomous systems (EPINOVA Working Paper No. EPINOVA-WP-A-2026-03). Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. https://doi.org/10.67037/epinova.wp.a.2026.003
related_works.description: Provides the author’s prior theoretical framework on morphological convergence, orchestration, and infrastructure-centered autonomous power.

related_works.identifier: Zhang, L., Ji, X., Jiao, Y., Qian, H., & Zhang, T. (2022). Design and control of the TransBoat: A transformable unmanned surface vehicle for overwater construction (arXiv:2212.03639). arXiv. https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.03639
related_works.description: Provides public research context showing that variable geometry is technically meaningful in transformable unmanned surface vehicle research.

tables.title: Core Metrics for Deployable Modular Platforms
tables.description: Proposes analytical metrics for assessing deployable modular surface platforms, including deployment ratio, functional interface ratio, modular separation index, distributed expansion coefficient, and interface maturity level.
tables.source: Author’s synthesis based on public U.S. Navy materials on containerized capability and unmanned surface vessels, NAVSEA’s Modular Attack Surface Craft program description, GAO’s assessment of Littoral Combat Ship modularity challenges, and the author’s prior framework on morphological convergence and infrastructure power in autonomous systems.
tables.note: The table shifts evaluation from platform capacity alone toward interface architecture and deployable mission-surface performance.
